pillar.metaTitle: Adult Day Care Centers Near Me | 1-800-MEDIGAP
pillar.metaDescription: Find adult day care centers near you, compare costs, and learn what Medicare covers. Get free help choosing senior day programs - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
pillar.h1: Adult Day Care Centers Near Me
pillar.quickAnswer: Adult day care centers provide supervised daytime care, meals, activities, and health monitoring for seniors who live at home. Costs average about $100 per day nationally (Genworth 2024). Original Medicare does not cover adult day care, but some Medicare Advantage and Medicaid waiver programs may. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.
pillar.intro: Adult day care centers let seniors stay active and supervised during the day while family caregivers work or rest. Here is how they work, what they cost, and how to find one near you.
pillar.heroSubhead: Compare local senior day programs, understand costs, and learn what Medicare and Medicaid pay. Free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What is an adult day care center?
sections.body: An adult day care center is a community-based program that provides supervised care, meals, social activities, and often health services for older adults during daytime hours. Seniors return home each evening. There are two main types: social day programs, which focus on activities and companionship, and adult day health care (ADHC), which adds nursing, therapy, and medication management for people with greater medical needs. These centers help seniors stay engaged and safe while giving family caregivers a needed break to work or rest. To compare programs near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How much do adult day care centers cost?
sections.body: The national median cost of adult day care is about $100 per day, or roughly $2,167 per month for five days a week, according to the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey. Prices vary widely by state and by whether the center offers basic social care or full adult day health care with nursing and therapy. Some programs charge hourly, and many offer sliding-scale fees based on income. Medicaid waivers, Veterans benefits, and long-term care insurance can offset the cost. Our licensed team can help you understand your options - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Does Medicare cover adult day care?
sections.body: Original Medicare (Parts A and B) does not cover adult day care, because it is considered custodial or non-medical care rather than skilled medical treatment. However, some Medicare Advantage (Part C) plans now offer adult day care or related supplemental benefits, and Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers cover it in many states for those who qualify financially. Coverage and benefits vary by plan and state. To check whether a Medicare Advantage plan in your area includes day-care benefits, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and speak with a licensed agent.

sections.h2: How do I find adult day care near me?
sections.body: Start with the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) from the U.S. Administration for Community Living, your local Area Agency on Aging, or the National Adult Day Services Association directory. When you call a center, ask about licensing, staff-to-participant ratios, medical services, transportation, meal options, and daily cost. Touring in person helps you judge cleanliness, safety, and how staff interact with participants. If a loved one has memory loss, ask specifically about dementia-trained staff and secured areas. For a free, personalized shortlist of options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Adult day care vs. respite care vs. rehab
sections.body: These services solve different problems. Adult day care is ongoing daytime supervision for seniors living at home. Respite care is temporary relief for a family caregiver - it can be a few hours, a day, or a short overnight stay. Rehabilitation (rehab) is short-term medical care to recover function after a hospital stay, surgery, illness, or injury, delivered in a facility or at home. Medicare covers rehab and limited inpatient respite under hospice, but generally not custodial day care. Sorting out which service fits your situation - and what insurance pays - is exactly what 1-800-MEDIGAP helps with.

faqs.q: What is the difference between adult day care and adult day health care?
faqs.a: Adult day care (social model) focuses on supervision, meals, and social activities. Adult day health care (ADHC) adds skilled services like nursing, physical or occupational therapy, and medication management for seniors with greater medical or cognitive needs. ADHC typically costs more and may qualify for Medicaid coverage in some states.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for adult day care centers?
faqs.a: Original Medicare does not pay for adult day care because it is custodial, non-skilled care. Some Medicare Advantage plans offer day-care or in-home support as supplemental benefits, and Medicaid waivers cover it for those who qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check plans in your area.

faqs.q: How much does adult day care cost per day?
faqs.a: The national median is about $100 per day, according to the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey, though prices range from roughly $50 to over $150 depending on your state and the level of care. Many centers offer sliding-scale fees, and Medicaid or VA benefits may reduce your cost.

faqs.q: Can adult day care help someone with dementia?
faqs.a: Yes. Many centers offer specialized memory-care programs with dementia-trained staff, structured routines, secured areas, and cognitive activities designed for Alzheimer's and other dementias. These programs keep seniors safe and engaged while giving caregivers a reliable daily break. Ask each center about its dementia-specific services.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for Medicaid coverage of adult day care?
faqs.a: Eligibility depends on your state's Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waiver. Generally you must meet financial limits and a functional or medical need for care. Each state sets its own rules, income limits, and waiting lists. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can point you to the right program.

faqs.q: Does adult day care include transportation?
faqs.a: Many adult day care centers provide door-to-door transportation, sometimes for an extra fee, while others coordinate with local senior transit services. Availability varies by center and by your distance from it. Always confirm transportation, scheduling, and any added cost before enrolling.

faqs.q: How many hours a day do adult day programs run?
faqs.a: Most adult day programs operate during standard business hours, typically 7 a.m. to 6 p.m., Monday through Friday, so they align with caregivers' work schedules. Some centers offer half-day options, weekend hours, or extended care. Confirm each center's exact hours and flexibility when you tour.

faqs.q: How do I find a licensed adult day care center near me?
faqs.a: Use the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), your Area Agency on Aging, or the National Adult Day Services Association directory. Verify state licensing, staff ratios, and services in person. For a free, personalized list of vetted options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.metaTitle: Adult Day Care Centers Near Me | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Find licensed adult day care centers near you, compare daily costs, and see what Medicare and Medicaid cover. Free help - call 1-800-MEDIGAP now.
page.h1: Adult Day Care Centers Near Me
page.quickAnswer: To find adult day care centers near you, search the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) or your local Area Agency on Aging. National median cost is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024). Original Medicare does not cover it, but Medicaid waivers and some Medicare Advantage plans may. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Looking for a trusted adult day care center nearby? Here is how to find one, what to ask, and what it costs.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local senior day programs and coverage options with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How to find adult day care centers near you
sections.body: Begin with the federal Eldercare Locator at eldercare.acl.gov, your local Area Agency on Aging, or the National Adult Day Services Association directory. These tools list licensed programs by ZIP code. When you contact a center, ask about state licensing, staff-to-participant ratios, medical and therapy services, meals, transportation, and the daily rate. Touring in person lets you assess cleanliness, safety, and how staff treat participants. For a free, personalized shortlist near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will help you compare options.

sections.h2: What to look for in a quality center
sections.body: A quality adult day care center is state-licensed, clearly staffed, and transparent about cost. Look for engaging daily activities, nutritious meals, medication management if needed, and trained staff - including dementia-specific care if your loved one has memory loss. Ask about secured exits, emergency procedures, and how the center communicates with families. Good centers welcome unannounced visits and provide a written agreement. If you want help vetting nearby centers or understanding which insurance benefits apply, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance at no cost.

faqs.q: What is the best way to find adult day care near me?
faqs.a: The Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) and your local Area Agency on Aging list licensed centers by ZIP code. The National Adult Day Services Association also offers a directory. For a free, personalized shortlist matched to your needs and budget, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much do adult day care centers near me cost?
faqs.a: The national median is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey), though local prices range from roughly $50 to over $150. Many centers offer sliding-scale fees, and Medicaid or VA benefits may lower your out-of-pocket cost.

faqs.q: Will Medicare pay for a local adult day care center?
faqs.a: Original Medicare does not cover adult day care. Some Medicare Advantage plans include it as a supplemental benefit, and Medicaid waivers cover it for those who qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check which plans in your area offer day-care benefits.

faqs.q: What questions should I ask when touring a center?
faqs.a: Ask about state licensing, staff ratios, medical and therapy services, meals, transportation, daily cost, dementia care, secured exits, and emergency procedures. Request a written agreement and confirm you can visit unannounced. A 1-800-MEDIGAP agent can give you a full checklist.

faqs.q: Do adult day care centers near me provide transportation?
faqs.a: Many do, sometimes for an added fee, while others coordinate with local senior transit. Availability depends on the center and your distance from it. Always confirm transportation and any extra cost before enrolling.

page.metaDescription: Find rehabilitation facilities near you and learn what Medicare covers for rehab after a hospital stay. Free guidance - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Rehabilitation facilities provide short-term therapy to help you recover after surgery, illness, stroke, or injury. Find them through Medicare's Care Compare tool (medicare.gov/care-compare). Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehab and skilled nursing rehab when criteria are met. For help understanding your coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Need rehab after a hospital stay? Here is how to find quality rehabilitation facilities nearby and what Medicare pays.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local rehab options and Medicare coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Types of rehabilitation facilities
sections.body: Rehabilitation comes in several settings. Inpatient rehabilitation facilities (IRFs) provide intensive therapy, usually at least three hours a day, for serious conditions like stroke or major surgery. Skilled nursing facilities (SNFs) offer less intensive rehab plus nursing care, often after a hospital stay. Outpatient rehab centers let you return home each day. Choosing the right setting depends on your medical needs and your doctor's recommendation. To compare facilities near you and understand what your Medicare plan covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, personalized guidance.

sections.h2: How to find and compare rehab facilities
sections.body: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare to find rehabilitation facilities and skilled nursing centers near you, complete with quality ratings and inspection data. Ask each facility about therapy intensity, staffing, specialties, discharge planning, and which insurance they accept. If you have Original Medicare plus a Medigap policy, your out-of-pocket rehab costs may be much lower than with Medicare Advantage. Sorting out which facility fits and what you will pay can be confusing - call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will walk you through it.

faqs.q: How do I find rehabilitation facilities near me?
faqs.a: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare to search by ZIP code and view quality ratings and inspection results. Your hospital discharge planner can also recommend facilities. For help comparing options and coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover rehabilitation facilities?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation and skilled nursing facility rehab when you meet eligibility criteria, such as a qualifying hospital stay and a doctor's order. Coverage has day limits and possible copays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your specific costs.

faqs.q: What is the difference between inpatient rehab and skilled nursing rehab?
faqs.a: Inpatient rehabilitation facilities provide intensive therapy, usually at least three hours daily, for serious conditions. Skilled nursing facilities offer lighter rehab combined with nursing care, often after a hospital stay. Your doctor recommends the right setting based on your condition and recovery goals.

faqs.q: How do I choose a good rehab facility?
faqs.a: Check Care Compare ratings, then ask about therapy intensity, staffing, specialties, discharge planning, and accepted insurance. Tour in person if possible and read recent inspection reports. A 1-800-MEDIGAP agent can help you weigh options and coverage.

faqs.q: Can a Medigap plan lower my rehab costs?
faqs.a: Yes. If you have Original Medicare, a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policy can cover coinsurance and deductibles for inpatient and skilled nursing rehab, reducing your out-of-pocket cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans available where you live.

page.metaDescription: Learn how short-term respite care gives family caregivers a break, what it costs, and what Medicare covers. Free help - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Short-term respite care gives family caregivers temporary relief by providing in-home help or a brief stay at a facility - from a few hours to a few weeks. Original Medicare covers respite only under the hospice benefit; otherwise costs are out of pocket or via Medicaid waivers. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for options.
page.intro: Short-term respite care lets caregivers rest, recover, or handle other obligations. Here is how it works and what it costs.
page.heroSubhead: Understand respite options and coverage with free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What short-term respite care includes
sections.body: Short-term respite care provides temporary relief for family caregivers. It can be delivered at home by an aide, in an adult day program, or as a brief overnight or multi-day stay at an assisted living or nursing facility. Respite can last a few hours, a full day, a weekend, or up to a few weeks while you travel, recover, or simply rest. The goal is to keep your loved one safe and cared for while preventing caregiver burnout. To find respite options near you and learn what your insurance covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How respite care is paid for
sections.body: Original Medicare covers short-term respite care only when a person is enrolled in hospice - it pays for up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite at a time. Outside hospice, respite is usually paid out of pocket, through Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers, Veterans benefits, long-term care insurance, or local nonprofit and Area Agency on Aging programs. Costs vary by setting and duration. Because the rules differ by program and state, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you find the most affordable path for your family.

faqs.q: What is short-term respite care?
faqs.a: Short-term respite care is temporary care that gives family caregivers a break - from a few hours to a few weeks. It can be provided at home, in an adult day program, or as a brief facility stay, keeping your loved one safe while you rest or attend to other needs.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover short-term respite care?
faqs.a: Original Medicare covers respite only under the hospice benefit, paying for up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite at a time. Outside hospice, respite is generally paid through Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, long-term care insurance, or out of pocket. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: How long can respite care last?
faqs.a: Respite ranges from a few hours to several weeks, depending on the program and your needs. Hospice respite is limited to up to five consecutive days per stay. In-home and facility respite outside hospice can be arranged for longer periods, subject to cost and availability.

faqs.q: Where can I get short-term respite care?
faqs.a: Respite is available through home care agencies, adult day centers, assisted living communities, and nursing facilities, as well as Area Agency on Aging programs. Use the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) to find providers, or call 1-800-MEDIGAP for personalized help.

faqs.q: How much does short-term respite care cost?
faqs.a: Costs depend on the setting. In-home respite often follows hourly home-aide rates, while facility respite may be billed daily, similar to assisted living or nursing rates. Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and nonprofits can offset costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore affordable options.

page.metaDescription: Find short term rehab near you after a hospital stay and learn what Medicare covers. Free help comparing facilities - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Short term rehab provides therapy and nursing to help you recover after surgery, illness, or a hospital stay, usually in a skilled nursing facility. Medicare Part A covers it after a qualifying 3-day inpatient stay, fully for days 1-20. Find facilities at medicare.gov/care-compare or call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Recovering from surgery or a hospital stay? Here is how to find short term rehab nearby and what Medicare pays.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local short term rehab and Medicare coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What short term rehab provides
sections.body: Short term rehab is goal-oriented recovery care, usually delivered in a skilled nursing facility (SNF), after surgery, a stroke, a fall, or another illness. It combines physical, occupational, and speech therapy with skilled nursing and help with daily activities. The aim is to restore strength and independence so you can return home safely, typically within days to a few weeks. To find well-rated short term rehab near you and confirm what your Medicare or Medigap plan covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, no-pressure guidance from a licensed agent.

sections.h2: Medicare coverage for short term rehab
sections.body: Medicare Part A covers short term rehab in a skilled nursing facility when you have a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days and a doctor orders skilled care. Medicare pays the full cost for days 1-20. For days 21-100, you pay a daily coinsurance ($209.50 per day in 2025) unless a Medigap plan covers it. After day 100, Medicare coverage ends. A Medigap policy can eliminate that coinsurance - call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare plans in your area.

faqs.q: How do I find short term rehab near me?
faqs.a: Search Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare by ZIP code to see skilled nursing facilities with rehab and their quality ratings. Your hospital discharge planner can also recommend options. For help comparing facilities and coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover short term rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers skilled nursing rehab after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay. It pays fully for days 1-20, then a daily coinsurance applies for days 21-100. A Medigap plan can cover that coinsurance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: How long is a typical short term rehab stay?
faqs.a: Most short term rehab stays last a few days to a few weeks, depending on your recovery and therapy goals. Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing care per benefit period when criteria are met, though many people go home well before that.

faqs.q: What is the difference between short term rehab and a nursing home?
faqs.a: Short term rehab is temporary, therapy-focused care aimed at recovery and returning home. A nursing home (long-term care) provides ongoing custodial care for people who cannot live independently. Both may occur in the same building, but Medicare covers only the skilled, short-term rehab.

faqs.q: How much will short term rehab cost me?
faqs.a: With Original Medicare, days 1-20 are fully covered. Days 21-100 carry a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) unless you have a Medigap plan that covers it. Costs differ under Medicare Advantage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your out-of-pocket cost.

page.metaDescription: See what adult day care costs per day and month, what affects the price, and what Medicaid covers. Free help finding options - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Adult day care costs a national median of about $100 per day, or roughly $2,167 per month for five days a week (Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey). Prices range from about $50 to over $150 daily by state and care level. Medicaid waivers and VA benefits can reduce cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Wondering what adult day care really costs? Here is a clear breakdown of daily and monthly prices and ways to pay.
page.heroSubhead: Understand adult day care pricing and assistance options with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What adult day care costs per day and month
sections.body: According to the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey, the national median for adult day care is about $100 per day, which works out to roughly $2,167 per month if attending five days a week. Actual prices range from about $50 to more than $150 per day depending on your state, the local cost of living, and whether the center offers basic social care or full adult day health care with nursing and therapy. Some centers bill hourly or offer half-day rates. For help comparing local prices, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Ways to lower adult day care costs
sections.body: Several programs can reduce what you pay. Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers cover adult day care for those who meet financial and care-need rules. Veterans may qualify through VA Aid and Attendance or adult day health care benefits. Long-term care insurance often includes adult day care, and many centers offer sliding-scale fees based on income. Local Area Agencies on Aging and nonprofits sometimes provide subsidies too. Because eligibility varies by program and state, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you find the most affordable path.

faqs.q: How much does adult day care cost per day?
faqs.a: The national median is about $100 per day, according to the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey. Depending on your state and the level of care, daily prices range from roughly $50 to over $150. Many centers offer sliding-scale fees or half-day rates.

faqs.q: How much does adult day care cost per month?
faqs.a: At the median rate of about $100 per day, five days a week costs roughly $2,167 per month. Your monthly total depends on how many days your loved one attends, your state, and whether the program includes health services like nursing or therapy.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid pay for adult day care?
faqs.a: Yes, in many states. Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers cover adult day care for people who meet financial and functional eligibility rules. Programs, income limits, and waiting lists vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help finding your state's program.

faqs.q: Is adult day care cheaper than home care or assisted living?
faqs.a: Often, yes. Adult day care's median of about $100 per day is generally less than full-time in-home care or assisted living for comparable supervision hours. It is a cost-effective option for seniors who live at home but need daytime support and social engagement.

faqs.q: Can VA benefits help pay for adult day care?
faqs.a: Yes. Eligible veterans may receive adult day health care through the VA or use the Aid and Attendance pension benefit toward day-care costs. Eligibility depends on service and care needs. A 1-800-MEDIGAP agent can help point you to the right VA resources.

page.metaDescription: Learn whether Medicare covers respite care, the hospice respite benefit, costs, and other options. Free guidance - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Medicare covers respite care only through the hospice benefit, paying for up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite at a time so a family caregiver can rest. You pay about 5% of the Medicare-approved cost. Outside hospice, Medicare does not cover respite. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for options.
page.intro: Wondering if Medicare pays for respite care? Here is exactly what is covered and what is not.
page.heroSubhead: Understand Medicare's respite rules and other funding options with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Medicare's hospice respite benefit
sections.body: Original Medicare covers respite care only for people enrolled in hospice. Under the hospice benefit, Medicare pays for short-term inpatient respite care - up to five consecutive days at a time - in a Medicare-approved facility, so the usual family caregiver can rest. You pay a small coinsurance, roughly 5% of the Medicare-approved amount for the respite stay. This benefit can be used more than once, though frequent use may be reviewed. To learn how the hospice respite benefit works for your family, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.

sections.h2: Respite options outside hospice
sections.body: If your loved one is not in hospice, Original Medicare does not cover respite care. Other options can help instead: Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers, Veterans benefits including Aid and Attendance, long-term care insurance, and programs through your local Area Agency on Aging. Some Medicare Advantage plans now offer in-home support or caregiver-relief benefits as supplemental coverage. Because availability and rules vary by plan and state, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you identify the respite resources you qualify for.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover respite care?
faqs.a: Medicare covers respite care only through the hospice benefit. It pays for up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite at a time so the family caregiver can rest, with you paying about 5% coinsurance. Outside hospice, Original Medicare does not cover respite.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare's hospice respite cost?
faqs.a: Under the hospice benefit, you pay roughly 5% of the Medicare-approved amount for inpatient respite care. The exact coinsurance depends on the facility's approved rate. There is no separate deductible for hospice respite. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help understanding your costs.

faqs.q: How many days of respite does Medicare hospice cover?
faqs.a: Medicare hospice covers up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite per stay. The benefit can be used more than once during hospice care, although repeated or frequent use may be reviewed by the hospice team based on need.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cover respite care?
faqs.a: Some Medicare Advantage plans offer caregiver-relief or in-home support as supplemental benefits, which may include limited respite. Coverage varies widely by plan and region. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check whether plans in your area include respite-related benefits.

faqs.q: What if my loved one is not in hospice - can I still get respite help?
faqs.a: Yes. Outside hospice, respite can be funded through Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, long-term care insurance, and Area Agency on Aging programs. These vary by state and eligibility. A 1-800-MEDIGAP agent can help you find respite resources you qualify for.

page.metaDescription: Learn whether Medicare covers rehab - inpatient, skilled nursing, and outpatient - plus costs and day limits. Free help - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Yes. Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation and skilled nursing facility rehab after a qualifying hospital stay, and Part B covers outpatient therapy. Skilled nursing rehab is fully covered for days 1-20, with coinsurance for days 21-100. Day limits and copays apply. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your costs.
page.intro: Need rehab after surgery, a stroke, or a fall? Here is what Medicare covers and what you will pay.
page.heroSubhead: Understand Medicare rehab coverage and lower your costs with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Types of rehab Medicare covers
sections.body: Medicare covers rehabilitation in several settings. Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) care for intensive therapy after serious conditions like stroke or major surgery, and it covers skilled nursing facility (SNF) rehab after a qualifying 3-day hospital stay. Part B covers outpatient physical, occupational, and speech therapy when medically necessary. Each setting has its own rules, day limits, and cost sharing. Knowing which type of rehab you qualify for - and what you will owe - can be confusing, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will explain your coverage clearly.

sections.h2: What you pay for Medicare rehab
sections.body: For skilled nursing rehab, Medicare pays the full cost for days 1-20, then charges a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) for days 21-100, after which coverage ends. Inpatient rehabilitation facility care follows the Part A hospital deductible and coinsurance structure. Outpatient therapy under Part B requires the Part B deductible plus 20% coinsurance. A Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan can cover much of this cost sharing, dramatically lowering your out-of-pocket expense. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans available where you live.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehab and skilled nursing facility rehab after a qualifying hospital stay, while Part B covers outpatient therapy. Coverage comes with day limits and cost sharing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand exactly what you will pay.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover inpatient rehabilitation?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) care for intensive therapy following serious conditions like stroke, spinal injury, or major surgery, when a doctor certifies you need it. The Part A deductible and coinsurance apply, which a Medigap plan can help cover.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare rehab cost?
faqs.a: Skilled nursing rehab is free for days 1-20, then $209.50 per day for days 21-100 in 2025. Inpatient rehab follows Part A deductible and coinsurance rules. Outpatient therapy carries 20% coinsurance. A Medigap plan can cover most of these costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover outpatient physical therapy?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part B covers medically necessary outpatient physical, occupational, and speech therapy. You pay the Part B deductible and 20% coinsurance. There is no longer a hard dollar cap, but therapy must remain medically necessary and documented by your provider.

faqs.q: Can a Medigap plan reduce my rehab costs?
faqs.a: Yes. With Original Medicare, a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan can cover the deductibles and coinsurance for inpatient, skilled nursing, and outpatient rehab, greatly lowering your out-of-pocket cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans in your area.

page.metaDescription: Find senior day programs near you, compare activities and costs, and learn what Medicaid covers. Free help choosing a program - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Senior day programs provide supervised daytime activities, meals, and socialization for older adults living at home. Find them via the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov) or your Area Agency on Aging. National median cost is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024). Medicaid waivers may help pay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Looking for engaging senior day programs nearby? Here is how to find one and what to expect.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local senior day programs and coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What senior day programs offer
sections.body: Senior day programs - also called adult day programs - give older adults a structured, supervised place to spend the day while living at home. Programs typically include social activities, exercise, meals, games, outings, and companionship, helping reduce isolation and keep seniors mentally and physically engaged. Some programs add health services like nursing or therapy for participants with greater needs. They also give family caregivers a reliable daytime break. To find well-rated senior day programs near you and learn what your insurance covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, personalized help.

sections.h2: Finding the right program near you
sections.body: Start with the Eldercare Locator at eldercare.acl.gov, your local Area Agency on Aging, or the National Adult Day Services Association directory to find programs by ZIP code. When you call, ask about activities, meals, transportation, daily cost, staff training, and whether the program serves participants with memory loss or specific health needs. Visiting in person helps you gauge the atmosphere and how staff interact with participants. For a free shortlist of programs matched to your loved one's needs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What are senior day programs?
faqs.a: Senior day programs, also called adult day programs, provide supervised daytime activities, meals, exercise, and socialization for older adults who live at home. Some include health services like nursing or therapy. They keep seniors engaged and give family caregivers a daytime break.

faqs.q: How do I find senior day programs near me?
faqs.a: Use the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), your Area Agency on Aging, or the National Adult Day Services Association directory to search by ZIP code. For a free, personalized list of programs matched to your needs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much do senior day programs cost?
faqs.a: The national median is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey), ranging from roughly $50 to over $150 by state and care level. Many programs offer sliding-scale fees, and Medicaid or VA benefits may reduce your cost.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for senior day programs?
faqs.a: Original Medicare does not cover senior day programs, since they are custodial rather than skilled care. Some Medicare Advantage plans include day-program benefits, and Medicaid waivers cover them for those who qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check options in your area.

faqs.q: Are senior day programs good for someone with memory loss?
faqs.a: Yes. Many programs offer memory-care tracks with dementia-trained staff, structured routines, and secured spaces designed for Alzheimer's and related dementias. These keep seniors safe and engaged while supporting caregivers. Ask each program about its memory-care services before enrolling.

page.metaDescription: See what respite care costs by setting - in-home, day, and facility - plus ways to pay. Free help finding affordable respite - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Respite care cost depends on the setting. In-home respite often runs at home-aide rates of about $30 per hour (Genworth 2024), adult day respite about $100 per day, and overnight facility respite at assisted living or nursing rates. Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and hospice can offset cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Wondering what respite care costs? Here is a clear breakdown by setting and how to make it affordable.
page.heroSubhead: Understand respite pricing and assistance options with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Respite care cost by setting
sections.body: Respite care is priced by where and how it is delivered. In-home respite from a home health aide runs at a national median of about $30 per hour (Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey), so a few hours of relief costs less than a full day. Adult day respite averages about $100 per day. Overnight or multi-day facility respite is billed at daily rates similar to assisted living (median about $5,900 per month) or nursing care. The right setting depends on your loved one's needs and how long you need relief. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare options.

sections.h2: How to lower respite care costs
sections.body: Several programs reduce respite costs. Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers cover respite for those who qualify financially. Veterans may access respite through VA benefits, including up to 30 days per year for eligible enrollees. Medicare's hospice benefit covers inpatient respite for hospice patients. Long-term care insurance, nonprofit grants, and Area Agency on Aging programs can also help. Because eligibility differs by program and state, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you find the most affordable respite path for your family.

faqs.q: How much does respite care cost?
faqs.a: It depends on the setting. In-home respite averages about $30 per hour (Genworth 2024), adult day respite about $100 per day, and overnight facility respite follows assisted living or nursing daily rates. Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and hospice can reduce your cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much does in-home respite care cost per hour?
faqs.a: In-home respite from a home health aide runs at a national median of about $30 per hour, according to the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey. Rates vary by state and agency. Some programs and benefits can offset this cost for qualifying families.

faqs.q: Does insurance help pay for respite care?
faqs.a: Yes, depending on your situation. Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, long-term care insurance, and Medicare's hospice respite benefit can all help. Original Medicare covers respite only under hospice. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn which programs you may qualify for.

faqs.q: Do veterans get free respite care?
faqs.a: Eligible veterans enrolled in VA health care may receive respite care, often up to 30 days per year, sometimes at little or no cost depending on their priority group and care needs. Contact your VA medical center or call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance on accessing it.

faqs.q: Is respite care cheaper than full-time care?
faqs.a: Yes. Because respite is temporary - a few hours, a day, or a short stay - it costs far less than ongoing full-time home care, assisted living, or nursing care. It is a cost-effective way to prevent caregiver burnout while keeping your loved one safe.

children.keyword: adult day care for dementia near me

page.metaTitle: Adult Day Care for Dementia Near Me | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Find adult day care for dementia near you with trained staff and secure settings. Learn costs and coverage. Free help - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.h1: Adult Day Care for Dementia Near Me
page.quickAnswer: Adult day care for dementia provides supervised, structured care with memory-trained staff and secured settings for seniors with Alzheimer's or other dementias. Find programs via the Eldercare Locator or Alzheimer's Association. National median cost is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024); Medicaid waivers may help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Caring for a loved one with dementia? Here is how to find safe, specialized adult day care nearby.
page.heroSubhead: Find dementia-friendly day programs and coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What dementia day care provides
sections.body: Adult day care for dementia is designed specifically for seniors with Alzheimer's and related memory conditions. These programs feature staff trained in dementia care, structured daily routines, secured exits to prevent wandering, calming environments, and activities that support cognition and dignity. Many offer health monitoring, meals, and personal-care assistance. The setting keeps your loved one safe and engaged while giving you a dependable daytime break to work or rest. To find dementia-specialized programs near you and understand what insurance covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, compassionate guidance.

sections.h2: Finding and paying for dementia day care
sections.body: Use the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), the Alzheimer's Association (alz.org or its 24/7 helpline), or your Area Agency on Aging to find dementia-focused day programs. When you call a center, ask about staff dementia training, secured areas, participant-to-staff ratios, and how they handle agitation or wandering. Costs run at a national median of about $100 per day (Genworth 2024), and Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, or long-term care insurance may help pay. For a free shortlist of dementia-friendly programs near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is adult day care for dementia?
faqs.a: Adult day care for dementia is a specialized daytime program for seniors with Alzheimer's or related conditions. It offers dementia-trained staff, structured routines, secured settings to prevent wandering, and engaging activities, keeping participants safe while giving caregivers a reliable break.

faqs.q: How do I find dementia day care near me?
faqs.a: Use the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), the Alzheimer's Association (alz.org), or your Area Agency on Aging to find memory-focused programs by ZIP code. For a free, personalized list of dementia-friendly day care near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much does dementia adult day care cost?
faqs.a: The national median is about $100 per day (Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey), though memory-care programs may cost slightly more for specialized staffing. Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and long-term care insurance can reduce your out-of-pocket cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

faqs.q: What should I ask a dementia day program?
faqs.a: Ask about staff dementia training, participant-to-staff ratios, secured exits, how they manage wandering and agitation, daily activities, meals, transportation, and cost. Request to tour and observe. A 1-800-MEDIGAP agent can provide a full checklist of questions.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover dementia day care?
faqs.a: In many states, yes. Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services waivers cover adult day health care, including dementia programs, for those who meet financial and care-need rules. Programs and waiting lists vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find your state's options.

children.keyword: what is respite care for seniors

page.metaTitle: What Is Respite Care for Seniors? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn what respite care for seniors is, the types available, costs, and what Medicare covers. Free guidance - call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.h1: What Is Respite Care for Seniors?
page.quickAnswer: Respite care for seniors is temporary care that gives a family caregiver a break - delivered at home, in an adult day program, or as a short facility stay. It ranges from a few hours to a few weeks. Original Medicare covers it only under hospice; Medicaid and VA benefits may help otherwise. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: New to the idea of respite care? Here is a plain-English explanation of what it is and how it helps.
page.heroSubhead: Understand respite care for seniors and your options with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Respite care explained
sections.body: Respite care is short-term, temporary care that gives family caregivers relief from the demands of caring for an older loved one. It allows the caregiver to rest, work, travel, or handle other responsibilities while a trained provider keeps the senior safe and well cared for. Respite can be planned in advance or arranged for an emergency. It is one of the most effective ways to prevent caregiver burnout, which affects a large share of the nation's roughly 53 million family caregivers (AARP). To explore respite options for your family, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Types of respite care for seniors
sections.body: Respite comes in several forms. In-home respite sends an aide or companion to the senior's home for a few hours or longer. Adult day programs offer daytime respite in a community setting with activities and meals. Residential or facility respite provides a short overnight or multi-day stay at an assisted living or nursing community. Hospice respite, covered by Medicare, offers up to five days of inpatient relief for hospice patients. Choosing the right type depends on your loved one's needs and how long you need a break. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: What is respite care for seniors?
faqs.a: Respite care for seniors is temporary care that gives a family caregiver a break. A trained provider cares for the older adult at home, in an adult day program, or in a short facility stay, ranging from a few hours to a few weeks, helping prevent caregiver burnout.

faqs.q: What are the types of respite care?
faqs.a: The main types are in-home respite (an aide comes to the home), adult day program respite, residential or facility respite (a short overnight stay), and hospice respite covered by Medicare. The right choice depends on your loved one's needs and how long you need relief.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover respite care for seniors?
faqs.a: Original Medicare covers respite only through the hospice benefit, paying for up to five consecutive days of inpatient respite at a time. Outside hospice, respite is usually funded by Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, long-term care insurance, or out of pocket. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Why is respite care important?
faqs.a: Respite prevents caregiver burnout, which is common among the roughly 53 million family caregivers in the U.S. (AARP). Regular breaks protect the caregiver's physical and mental health, improve the quality of care, and help families sustain caregiving at home longer.

faqs.q: How do I arrange respite care?
faqs.a: Start with the Eldercare Locator (eldercare.acl.gov), your Area Agency on Aging, home care agencies, or local adult day programs. Plan ahead when possible, and ask about cost and funding. For personalized help arranging respite, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.metaDescription: Find inpatient rehab facilities near you and learn what Medicare covers after a hospital stay. Free help comparing options - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Inpatient rehab facilities provide intensive therapy - usually at least three hours a day - for serious recovery after stroke, surgery, or injury. Find them via Medicare's Care Compare (medicare.gov/care-compare). Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehab under hospital rules. For help with coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Need intensive rehab after a serious medical event? Here is how to find inpatient rehab nearby and what Medicare pays.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local inpatient rehab and Medicare coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What inpatient rehab provides
sections.body: An inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) delivers intensive, hospital-level therapy for people recovering from serious conditions such as stroke, spinal cord injury, brain injury, major surgery, or multiple trauma. Patients typically receive at least three hours of therapy a day, five days a week, from a coordinated team of physicians, nurses, and physical, occupational, and speech therapists. The goal is to restore as much function and independence as possible before returning home. To find inpatient rehab near you and understand your Medicare coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, personalized guidance from a licensed agent.

sections.h2: Medicare coverage and finding a facility
sections.body: Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation facility care when a doctor certifies you need intensive therapy and 24-hour rehabilitation nursing. Costs follow the Part A hospital deductible and coinsurance structure per benefit period, which a Medigap plan can largely cover. To find facilities, use Medicare's Care Compare at medicare.gov/care-compare, which shows quality ratings and outcomes, or ask your hospital discharge planner. Comparing facilities and understanding your share of the cost can be confusing - call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will help you sort it out.

faqs.q: How do I find inpatient rehab near me?
faqs.a: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare to search inpatient rehabilitation facilities by ZIP code and view quality ratings. Your hospital discharge planner can also recommend facilities. For help comparing options and coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover inpatient rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers inpatient rehabilitation facility care when a doctor certifies you need intensive therapy and 24-hour rehab nursing. Costs follow the Part A deductible and coinsurance structure, which a Medigap plan can help cover. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: What conditions qualify for inpatient rehab?
faqs.a: Inpatient rehab commonly serves recovery from stroke, spinal cord or brain injury, major surgery such as joint replacement, amputation, and multiple trauma. You must be able to tolerate intensive therapy - usually about three hours a day - and a doctor must certify the need.

faqs.q: How is inpatient rehab different from a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: Inpatient rehab provides more intensive therapy (about three hours daily) with hospital-level medical oversight, suited to serious conditions. A skilled nursing facility offers lighter therapy plus nursing care, often for slower recoveries. Your doctor recommends the right setting for your needs.

faqs.q: How much does inpatient rehab cost with Medicare?
faqs.a: Inpatient rehab follows Medicare Part A's hospital deductible and coinsurance per benefit period. A Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan can cover most of that cost sharing, greatly reducing your out-of-pocket expense. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans in your area.

page.metaDescription: Find skilled nursing rehab near you and learn what Medicare covers after a hospital stay. Free help comparing facilities - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Skilled nursing rehab provides therapy and 24-hour nursing in a skilled nursing facility, usually after a hospital stay. Find facilities at medicare.gov/care-compare. Medicare Part A covers it fully for days 1-20 after a qualifying 3-day inpatient stay, then coinsurance applies. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.
page.intro: Recovering after a hospital stay? Here is how to find quality skilled nursing rehab nearby and what Medicare covers.
page.heroSubhead: Compare local skilled nursing rehab and Medicare coverage with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What skilled nursing rehab includes
sections.body: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) provides short-term rehab combined with 24-hour skilled nursing care, typically after a hospital stay for surgery, a stroke, a fall, or another illness. Care includes physical, occupational, and speech therapy, wound care, IV medications, and help with daily activities, all overseen by nurses and your physician. The goal is to recover enough strength and independence to return home safely. To find well-rated skilled nursing rehab near you and confirm what Medicare or your Medigap plan covers, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, personalized guidance.

sections.h2: Medicare coverage for skilled nursing rehab
sections.body: Medicare Part A covers skilled nursing rehab when you have a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days and a doctor orders skilled care. Medicare pays the full cost for days 1-20. For days 21-100, you owe a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) unless a Medigap plan covers it. Coverage ends after day 100 per benefit period. Use medicare.gov/care-compare to compare facility ratings. To lower your coinsurance with a Medigap plan, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How do I find skilled nursing rehab near me?
faqs.a: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare to search skilled nursing facilities by ZIP code and review quality ratings and inspection results. Your hospital discharge planner can also recommend facilities. For help comparing options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover skilled nursing rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part A covers skilled nursing rehab after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, paying fully for days 1-20. Days 21-100 carry a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025), which a Medigap plan can cover. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: How long does Medicare pay for skilled nursing rehab?
faqs.a: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing care per benefit period when criteria are met - fully for days 1-20, then with daily coinsurance for days 21-100. After day 100, coverage ends until a new benefit period begins. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

faqs.q: What is the 3-day rule for skilled nursing rehab?
faqs.a: Medicare requires a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days (not counting the discharge day) before it will cover skilled nursing facility rehab. Time spent under observation status does not count. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you have questions about your stay.

faqs.q: Can a Medigap plan cover skilled nursing coinsurance?
faqs.a: Yes. Several Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plans cover the skilled nursing facility daily coinsurance for days 21-100, eliminating that out-of-pocket cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans available where you live and find one that fits your budget.

children.keyword: how long does medicare pay for rehab

page.metaTitle: How Long Does Medicare Pay for Rehab? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how long Medicare pays for rehab - skilled nursing, inpatient, and outpatient - plus day limits and costs. Free help - call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: How Long Does Medicare Pay for Rehab?
page.quickAnswer: Medicare pays for skilled nursing rehab up to 100 days per benefit period - fully for days 1-20, then a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) for days 21-100. Inpatient rehab follows Part A hospital benefit-period limits. Outpatient therapy continues while medically necessary. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.
page.intro: Worried about running out of Medicare rehab coverage? Here is exactly how long Medicare pays, by setting.
page.heroSubhead: Understand Medicare's rehab day limits and lower your costs with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Skilled nursing rehab day limits
sections.body: For skilled nursing facility (SNF) rehab, Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days per benefit period when you meet the rules, including a qualifying 3-day hospital stay. Medicare pays the full cost for days 1-20. For days 21-100, you owe a daily coinsurance - $209.50 in 2025 - unless a Medigap plan covers it. After day 100, Medicare stops paying until a new benefit period begins, which requires 60 days without inpatient or skilled care. To avoid surprise costs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will explain your coverage.

sections.h2: Inpatient and outpatient rehab limits
sections.body: Inpatient rehabilitation facility care is paid under the Part A hospital benefit. Within one benefit period, Medicare fully covers days 1-60 after the deductible, then charges daily coinsurance for days 61-90, with 60 lifetime reserve days beyond that. Outpatient therapy under Part B has no hard day or dollar limit - Medicare keeps paying its share (you owe 20% after the deductible) as long as the therapy stays medically necessary and your provider documents it. A Medigap plan can cover much of this cost sharing - call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare plans.

faqs.q: How long does Medicare pay for rehab?
faqs.a: It depends on the setting. Skilled nursing rehab is covered up to 100 days per benefit period (free for days 1-20, coinsurance for 21-100). Inpatient rehab follows Part A hospital limits. Outpatient therapy continues while medically necessary. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for specifics.

faqs.q: How many days of skilled nursing rehab does Medicare cover?
faqs.a: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing rehab per benefit period when criteria are met - fully for days 1-20, then with a $209.50 daily coinsurance (2025) for days 21-100. After 100 days, coverage ends until a new benefit period begins.

faqs.q: What happens after Medicare rehab coverage runs out?
faqs.a: Once you exceed the covered days, you pay the full cost yourself unless you have a Medigap plan, Medicaid, or other coverage. A new benefit period - and renewed coverage - begins after 60 days without inpatient or skilled care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to plan ahead.

faqs.q: Is there a limit on outpatient physical therapy?
faqs.a: No hard dollar cap exists anymore. Medicare Part B keeps paying its share of medically necessary outpatient therapy (you owe 20% after the deductible), but above a threshold your provider must document that continued therapy is needed. Coverage continues as long as it remains medically necessary.

faqs.q: How can I avoid paying rehab coinsurance?
faqs.a: A Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan can cover the skilled nursing and hospital coinsurance Medicare leaves you, so you owe little or nothing for covered rehab days. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare Medigap plans available in your area and find one that fits your budget.
